skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, editing, or testing AI skills before deployment with a test-driven documentation workflow.
skill.summary: Guides test-driven skill authoring, pressure testing, and skill documentation quality checks.
skill.author: Cygnusfear

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown code fences, documentation examples, URLs, and terms inside authoring guidance. The confirmed risk is a bundled Node.js helper that invokes Graphviz through child_process and writes SVG or DOT output files, so publication should include a warning about local script execution.

medium_findings.title: Local Graphviz Execution Helper
medium_findings.description: render-graphs.js imports child_process and runs Graphviz with execSync to render DOT diagrams from a skill directory. The command name is fixed and DOT content is passed through stdin, so I did not find evidence of shell injection, but running this helper executes a local binary on untrusted diagram input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly imports child_process and calls execSync. Confidence is high for local command execution, while malicious intent is not confirmed because the command is fixed and used for diagram rendering.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes From Helper Script
medium_findings.description: render-graphs.js reads a target SKILL.md file, creates a diagrams directory, and writes SVG and DOT files under the user-supplied skill directory. This is legitimate for a rendering utility, but marketplace users should know the skill includes a script that can modify files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read, directory creation, and file writes are explicit in the helper. The output path is under the selected skill directory and generated names are constrained, so the risk appears moderate rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Agent Persuasion Guidance Requires Careful Use
medium_findings.description: The skill includes material on authority, commitment, pressure scenarios, and compliance language for skill design. I did not find instructions that target the auditor or tell a model to skip safety analysis, but this content can influence agent behavior if reused without care.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The persuasive intent is documented clearly, but it is framed as skill testing and documentation design. Confidence is medium because this is a behavioral-content risk, not confirmed malicious code.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified As Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick findings are false positives. The cited content is Markdown code fences, inline file names, examples, or prose inside documentation rather than executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documents and the sampled lines show fenced examples or prose. No Ruby runtime file or executable Ruby command path was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misclassified As Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are false positives for runtime network access. The URLs appear in Markdown links and image references copied from authoring documentation, and no code fetches those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation links or image tags. I found no runtime HTTP client usage connected to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Skill Paths Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden home directory findings refer to examples about checking Claude skill directories. They do not perform hidden file access by themselves, but users should treat copied shell examples as actions that may inspect local skill folders.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are plain Markdown examples mentioning ~/.claude/skills. No code automatically reads those paths in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And C2 Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and C2 keyword findings appear to come from ordinary words, Markdown file names, URL hashes, or documentation text. I did not find evidence of cryptographic code, command-and-control behavior, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sampled lines contain skill documentation, image URLs, file naming guidance, or Graphviz comments. I found no code that implements weak cryptography or external command-and-control communication.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process execSync Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The bundled Node.js script uses execSync to call local system commands for Graphviz rendering. This is a known sensitive pattern and should only be run on trusted skill directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The child_process import and execSync calls are explicit. The commands are fixed, so this is a dangerous pattern warning rather than confirmed command injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Read And Write Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper reads SKILL.md from a supplied directory and writes rendered diagram files. These operations are expected for the utility, but they expand the skill beyond passive documentation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fs calls are direct and easy to verify. The behavior is bounded to the selected skill directory in normal use, lowering severity.

content.user_title: Create and Test AI Writing Skills
content.value_statement: Many AI skills fail because their instructions are not tested under realistic pressure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for writing, testing, and refining skills before deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to write skill descriptions that trigger on use cases instead of workflow summaries.
Maps test-driven development concepts to skill creation and documentation testing.
Provides pressure-test formats for checking whether agents follow skill instructions.
Documents common rationalizations agents use when skipping required workflows.
Includes Graphviz style guidance and a local helper for rendering skill flowcharts.
References official Anthropic skill authoring guidance and supporting examples.
limitations: It is focused on AI skill writing, not general prose editing or copywriting.
The testing workflow assumes access to agents or subagents for pressure scenarios.
The optional diagram renderer requires Node.js and a local Graphviz installation.
Some bundled reference material is long and may need selective reading.

use_cases.title: Author a New Skill
use_cases.description: Create a skill with clear triggers, concise structure, and supporting files only when needed.

use_cases.title: Validate Agent Compliance
use_cases.description: Run pressure scenarios to verify that an agent follows a skill under time, authority, or sunk-cost pressure.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Skill Documentation
use_cases.description: Find unclear descriptions, weak examples, missing tests, and rationalization gaps before release.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Skill Description
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a concise description for a new skill about [topic]. Focus only on when the skill should trigger.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Skill Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my SKILL.md for structure, trigger clarity, token efficiency, and missing supporting files.

prompt_templates.title: Design Pressure Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create three pressure scenarios that test whether agents follow [skill name] under realistic constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor After Failed Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to analyze these failed pressure-test transcripts and propose targeted edits that close each rationalization.

output_examples.input: Review a draft skill for flaky test debugging.
output_examples.output: A concise review that flags a workflow-heavy description, recommends trigger-focused wording, and lists pressure tests for timing failures.

output_examples.input: Create pressure scenarios for a verification-before-completion skill.
output: A time-pressure scenario with explicit choices.
A sunk-cost scenario that tests whether the agent skips verification.
A meta-test prompt that asks why the agent ignored the skill.

output_examples.input: Improve a skill after agents keep rationalizing exceptions.
output_examples.output: A targeted edit plan with a new core principle, a rationalization table, and a short retest checklist.

best_practices: Run baseline pressure tests before writing or changing a skill.
Keep the description focused on triggers, not the skill workflow.
Use supporting files only for heavy references, reusable tools, or complete examples.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill first and testing it only after deployment.
Putting long process summaries in the description field.
Creating many weak examples instead of one strong, reusable example.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create and test AI skills, especially skills used by Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it write general marketing copy?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on skill documentation, trigger descriptions, testing workflows, and supporting references.

faq.question: Why does it use test-driven development terms?
faq.answer: It treats skill instructions like process code. You first observe failure, then write targeted guidance, then retest.

faq.question: Does the skill include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an optional Node.js helper that renders Graphviz diagrams and writes output files locally.

faq.question: Is the diagram helper required?
faq.answer: No. The main documentation workflow can be used without running the helper script.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing?
faq.answer: Users should review pressure-test results, description clarity, supporting files, and any optional script execution.
